items.q: What does a mole of a substance represent?
items.correct: Avogadro's number (6.02 x 10^23) of particles of that substance.
items.misconception: A mole is a unit of mass equal to the relative molecular mass in grams of any substance.
items.random_wrong: A mole is a unit of volume equal to 24 dm^3 at any temperature.
items.why: Students conflate the mole (a count) with molar mass (a mass-per-mole), because in practice they use moles to convert to grams.

items.q: What is different between two isotopes of the same element?
items.correct: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons, which is why their mass differs.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes are atoms of different elements with the same mass.
items.why: Students conflate isotopes with ions because both alter a property of the atom — but ions change electrons (charge), isotopes change neutrons (mass).

items.q: What happens to electrons when sodium reacts with chlorine?
items.correct: Sodium loses one electron to chlorine; the resulting Na+ and Cl- ions attract electrostatically.
items.misconception: Sodium and chlorine share an electron between them.
items.random_wrong: Protons move from chlorine to sodium to balance the charges.
items.why: Students over-apply the covalent (sharing) model to all bonding because sharing is introduced first and feels intuitive.

items.q: The formula of calcium chloride is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: CaCl2. “CaCl” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Balance: Na + Cl2 -> NaCl. The balanced equation is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2Na + Cl2 -> 2NaCl. “Na + Cl2 -> NaCl” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Calculate Mr of CaCO3 (Ca=40, C=12, O=16).
items.why: Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
